I strongly agree with calling this `--resolve` and having it generic, rather than `--py-resolve`. That is good alignment with `generate-lockfiles`. I also generally am in favor of only supporting export when you use resolves. NB that that will still work even if you use manually generated lockfiles, like via `poetry export`! (A good thing). Given the security importance, I'm generally more and more in favor of how we can push people to use resolves.

My only uncertainty is what `./pants export` should do with no args. Right now, I think it no-ops? Should we instead be like `generate-lockfiles` that we export every resolve? If we didn't have the CLI specs legacy, would that change the answer?

I think this is pretty good. I might shorten or consolidate or lower from critical the logging calls, since there's now two copies of them and they're kind of complicated. I think we must have added them for debugging originally and left them in.

As for whether `EPERM` is the only other code we need to handle, figuring that out kind of *is* the issue here. The underlying call is going to be `link()` from section 2 of the man pages, on Linux, and  [`man 2 link`](https://linux.die.net/man/2/link) lists a bunch of errors that can be returned besides those we handle here. These include things like `ENOMEM` which is returned if the kernel runs out of memory when trying to make the link, so it's debatable whether most of them can really be recovered from.

One that might be worth handling is `EMLINK`, since there is a limit on the total number of hardlinks a file can have to it, and someone might manage to hit it.

On Mac, the [Mac man page for link](https://developer.apple.com/library/archive/documentation/System/Conceptual/ManPages_iPhoneOS/man2/link.2.html) (which is also the iOS one?)  doesn't seem to list any different errors that can happen.

Let's split install-dev.sh into two parts that can be either executed at once (with no option) or separately (with `--codespace-on-create`, `--codespace-post-create` so that we can use prebuilt containers.

- In "on create", run `docker pull compose` and `./pants export ::` to pre-download and populate external dependencies that takes long time to fetch and install.
- In "post create", run any configuration and cloning of additional repositories because in this stage we have fully configured GitHub access tokens.
- If run inside Codespace (we could check this by environment variables), we may skip several prerequisite checks (such as availability of Docker).
- Let's explicitly add a handful range (about 10?) of forwarded TCP ports for wsproxy and explicitly configure the wsproxy worker port range as well (for v1).

The latest commits add the following changes:

- Following #7170, we no longer need special handling for any non-image parts of the samples, since all our datasets fit the heuristic.
- Following https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/7171#issuecomment-1419241405 we no longer use the `datapoints.Label` class. Together with the pass-through for PIL images, this means that this wrapper is a no-op for classification datasets. This also resolves all discussions regarding the categories, since they are no longer present.
- Instead of having a wrapper that takes the dataset as well as the sample as inputs, i.e.
  
  ```python
  def wrapper(dataset, sample):
      ...
      return wrapped_sample
  ```

  the architecture was changed to a factory pattern:

  ```python
  def wrapper_factory(dataset):
      def wrapper(sample):
          ...
          return wrapped_sample
  
      return wrapper
  ```

  In addition to making the wrapper more "natural", this also enables us to raise on unsupported behavior on wrapping rather than when the first sample is drawn. That should improve UX.
- I've added automated smoke tests to our datasets v1 tests to make sure wrapping and drawing samples doesn't raise anything.

> This is awesome, really cool work!

Thanks! Glad to finally be able to contribute!

> For raw output, I agree with the decision to simply print the whole BUILD file. Way too tricky to otherwise try to identify which part is applicable. And we hope/expect BUILD files to be fairly small thanks to dep inference.

I played (a very small bit) with some inspection tools to find the applicable part, but it looked complicated. It could be an issue in its own right if we wanted to pursue it more.
 
> However, I could see it getting confusing when you specify multiple targets/files, and mapping what belongs to what. Thoughts on detecting the case of >1 "spec", and logging a warning to consider using the JSON format? We do that with `./pants py-constraints`:

I don't think a warning is necessary if we make JSON the default output, but I'll defer to your judgement on this.

> The JSON output is excellent: it shows the user what Pants's view of a particular file/target actually is. That transparency is great for debugging. We might want to make JSON the default?
> 
> I'd recommend two tweaks:
> 
>     1. Rather than `alias`, call it `target_type`, or something similar. "Alias" is an implementation detail.
> 
>     2. I think we want to render default values? That way, we show a complete view of what Pants is doing. It also allows for safer JSON queries, as you're guaranteed the key will exist. If it gets too chatty, we could consider an option like `--ignore-defaults`.
> 

I agree with these suggestions.

- [ ] Make JSON the default output style
- [ ] Rename `alias` in output to `target_type`
- [ ] Show default values by default
- [ ] Add `--exclude-defaults` flag (optional)

> In #4861, we mentioned adding a table format in a followup. I'm not sure that would really be necessary - the mix of Raw and JSON is very helpful already.

Agreed. It's human-readable enough, and machine readable with things like `jq`

> In #4861, I pondered
> 
> > Should [we] evaluate the sources and dependencies fields, or show the raw values?
> 
> After seeing this PR, I think no, we should not. `./pants dependencies` already exists for deps, and `./pants filedeps` for sources. It's redundant imo, and also non-trivial, to include those here, including a performance hit.

Agreed, and if you really want that, `jq` + `xargs` are your friends:

```
./pants peek ... | jq -r ... | xargs ./pants filedeps
```

Although I admit that trying to come up with a good example `jq` query made me realize that it doesn't seem to play well with arbitrary keys like we're using, so perhaps making a list of objects is a better way forward. Any opinion on this, @Eric-Arellano ?

I think these changes do address the request in #812 and the added tests cover that new behavior well. Nice work!

However, I have one more question. Still thinking about latency-sensitive users (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4909#pullrequestreview-839729178), I feel that the default value for `validate_features` should be `False`.

As a user of a machine learning framework, I think I'd prefer to be told

> *"hey this method can now do some extra validation, change your code to opt into it"* 

instead of

> *"hey this method now does some extra validation by default, change your code to avoid the performance penalty of that validation"*

I feel like that specifically for `predict()` methods since those are necessary for serving models and therefore latency-sensitive. I expect other methods related to training, cross validation, or plotting (for example) to be much less latency-sensitive.

Before making changes, let's also hear what @StrikerRUS and @shiyu1994 hear about this topic.

My preference would be to have tests that actually test what the writers are doing and what they are responsible for. The CF tests should test that the proper conversions are happening. The writer tests don't need to make sure that CF conversion for every case happened because the CF tests should have covered that.

In the cases where you want to make sure that something the CF conversion is doing is saved/rendered properly on-disk then I think putting it in a writer test is fine, but you could also use `to_netcdf` and `to_zarr` directly...maybe. I'm not sure how much I'll regret saying that later.

Most of this looks fine to me. I left some inline comments trying to address some of the questions you had. I think in general if you think some functions could be extracted and/or shared then go for it.

I think I requested this somewhere else, but I'd really like if we could be more specific with function names and module names regarding what is private (`_` prefix) and public. For example, if a module in `satpy/writers/cf/` is only used internally to that sub-package/module then I think the module name could start with a `_`. Similarly, if a function is only used inside its own module then it should get a `_` prefix to make sure users/readers don't assume it can be imported into somewhere else. The opposite of this is that you shouldn't be importing a function with a `_` from one module to another. Thoughts?

Yeah, I think this looks pretty good!  I noticed a couple remaining issues though:

`self.exception_flag` is written in a thread-unsafe manner now, via `try_request` and `clean_after_success`.  I'd suggest simplifying that code by folding `clean_after_success` into `try_request` since there's not any real gain in having it separate now, and then modifying `try_request` to set a local variable to true/false for the exception state, and adding a  simple block at the end of the function like this:

```
    self.lock.acquire(True)
    self.exception_flag = local_exception_flag
    self.lock.release()
```

Next, the locking in `try_lock_and_do_flush_request` seems to be broken in that it is possible to have the following sequence:

1. Caller calls `buffer_log_sync` which acquires the lock
2. Buffer size after adding the message exceeds `self.flush_limit` so `buffer_log_sync` calls `self.flush()`
3. `self.flush` calls `schedule_flush_sync`
4. `schedule_flush_sync` fails to queue the flush via the request thread pool for some reason
5. `schedule_flush_sync` directly calls `try_lock_and_do_flush_request`
6. `try_lock_and_do_flush_request` fails to get the lock because it is still held in `buffer_log_sync`
7. `buffer_log_sync` fails to flush the request and instead tries to kill and restart the flusher, which doesn't seem desirable.

Thanks for taking this on!  Code LGTM, just some comments on the code comments and tests.  I do think this makes the Time Series visualization more correct, even if it's hard to actually generate a case where it is visually different.

FWIW - I expected to be able to get this to visually look different by changing the demo's `single_value` example to be ten 0.0 values.  This should have triggered the "wrong" treatment (two peaks of count 5, rather than one peak of count 10), because this code would generate 30 intervals from -1.0 to 1.0, and right at the middle, the 0.0 values would fall on the middle boundary and get split 0.5/0.5.

However, that doesn't happen, which I eventually figured out is due to floating point error 🤦‍♂️   The bins generated by the logic in that case actually look like this:

```
0: {x: -1, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 0}
1: {x: -0.9333333333333333, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 0}
...
14: {x: -0.06666666666666665, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 10}
15: {x: 0, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 0}
...
29: ​{x: 0.9333333333333333, dx: 0.06666666666666667, y: 0}
```

Critically, although the `x` for bin 15 is exactly 0, for bin 14, `x = 0.0666...5` while `dx = 0.0666...7`, so they don't quite add up to `0.0`:

```
>>> -0.06666666666666665 + 0.06666666666666667
1.3877787807814457e-17
```

As a result, because it sees that `0.0 < 1.38777...e-17`, doesn't split them evenly after all (it does technically put them in the "wrong" bin, but visually that's less important).

Anyway, maybe at some point I'll try to fix this by ensuring we actually use the same exact values for left/right edges of adjacent bins, since at least in theory having two different calculations could mean we double-count or incorrectly omit counts.

The main issue I see is in `_apply_norm`. If the perturbation has any `inf` or `-inf` values in it, the resultant vector will be all 0s and NaNs:
```
>>> grad
array([-inf, -10.,  -1.,   0.,   1.,  10.,  inf,   0.])
>>> grad / (np.sum(np.abs(grad), axis=ind, keepdims=True) + tol)
array([nan, -0., -0.,  0.,  0.,  0., nan,  0.])
>>> grad / (np.sqrt(np.sum(np.square(grad), axis=ind, keepdims=True)) + tol)
array([nan, -0., -0.,  0.,  0.,  0., nan,  0.])
```

Fixing this problem is difficult, as you need to define `inf / inf`, but at the least you should have a warning if infinite values appear in the gradient.

Hey, thanks a lot for the PR and the time it took to implement this.

I'm going to be blunt and extremely apologetic for a second and say that I'm not going to accept this PR for a few reasons.

1. The scope is way too broad.
2. The development of these features requires a lot of design discussion.
3. The development of these features are already planned and are in the process of being discussed and developed.
4. Current focus is in 1.7 which will release soon, 2.0 development will start right afterwards.

For starters, the change from v7 to v8 is a bit more subtle and it breaks things in ways that haven't been covered here (more on that later in the actual review). For example, certain cache policies break in v8 due to subtle changes in certain gateway events like ``PRESENCE_UPDATE``.

Secondly, the design of slash commands that I have in mind currently requires a Python version bump from 3.5 to something like 3.7 or 3.8 and will hopefully be a rich design that allows for slash commands to be implemented in a natural manner. I'm also planning on splitting it from `discord.Client` so a user who doesn't actually want to use slash commands doesn't have to pay for the cost of the state tracking involved with them.

I'm currently working on a slash command implementation and I need to envision a proper design for them that feels right to use so I haven't been in a rush.

I really do appreciate the time and effort it took to write this though, and if you'd be willing to aid in development in the future please feel free to join the [discord server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ) so we can do the required discussion for any future contribution, if you're willing, there. 

If you want to reach out and see how development is going for the slash commands project you can also join and reach out -- I'd be happy to discuss it with you.

I don't think this is necessary, based onhttps://github.com/taskcluster/taskcluster/pull/3615#issuecomment-715636601.

I suspect this PR was created because of #3691, with the idea that if one service needs this scope, they all do.

I think the only reason that #3691 was required was that #3616 landed before the rest of the RFC#165 work in #3615.  In fact, I think that both PRs become redundant after #3615 lands and can be reverted.  If #3615 is correct, then we *never* need to specify `assume:anonymous` anywhere, because it's implicitly included everywhere (including in `auth.currentScopes` which the frontend uses for the Profile view).

So to be clear, my thinking is:
 - close this without landing it
 - include a revert of #3616 and #3691 in #3615.

As per DM discussions - this snapshot mutation approach is very concerning due to it changing the immutable nature of the snapshot objects. It is very likely that we will leverage the immutable nature of snapshots for things like caching in the future and accounting for this type of complexity would make that very difficult. 

As was called out in the summary, an approach roughly like 

> Is there some other mutable object we need to create that can store cross-repo dependencies that can be referenced from the graphql layer?

is much more appealing. `PipelineIndex` is an example of an object that layers over immutable snapshots to support efficient access patterns in host processes. 

I also think it is worth revisiting how asset nodes are modeled at the graphql layer to account for this problem. It's possible that something like having separate top level nodes and edges/dependencies information instead of just top level list of nodes where the nodes know their edges/deps could be easier to merge across multiple repositories.

Argh, I knew there was a reason I marked SBU in the tracker issue. When I gave you the go I just saw that there is something to download and assumed I was mistaken before. That is on me, my bad.

In general your solution looks really good and works, but has one major downside: We need to re-download every image for every iteration. While we plan to support streaming datasets from the internet, we are not there yet. Thus, we need to download everything.

That could be achieved with a [`OnDiskCacheHolder`](https://github.com/pytorch/data/blob/2f66c15deeadebfd10d4893553e9ba8d8de52190/torchdata/datapipes/iter/util/cacheholder.py#L114), but that would mean we would only download at runtime. All current datasets download everything upfront and I would keep it that way for now.

My solution is to put a custom `preprocess` method onto the resource and download everything there.

Just changed the messages based on what we talked about in Slack.

A few requests - 

* please move the .json DebugScripts to ai2thor/unity/debug_scripts or ai2thor/unity/scripts/debug.  Anything in the Unity special folders (https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/SpecialFolders.html) gets imported as an asset into the asset database.  Unity also watches for any changes to these files, so there is some overhead.  These types of files should live outside the UnityEditor owned directories.

* Instead of using a base path, look at using https://docs.unity3d.com/ScriptReference/EditorUtility.OpenFilePanel.html.  You can then provide a default starting directory for users to select a json file and if they want to load a script from outside the project, they can also do that.  This makes it more convenient if they just want to try something and they have a file living in a different location and don't want to figure out what directory they need to drop the json file in for it to get executed.  As well, this eliminates any chance of typos since they either select a json file or not.

* You are right about Debug.Break.  I used the step frame feature in conjunction with python connected to the editor.  This allowed me to step through actions in python one at a time and view the changes in the editor.  I tried several times to get the Break to trigger before a command got executed, but could not.  I imagine we could come up with some way to get around the behavior but it doesn't seem like all the complexity would improve upon just breaking with a debugger which will actually stop at a breakpoint.  

* The primary reason for using ExecuteBatch + StartCoroutine is that the current way that actions are executed the Unity lifecycle is being skipped for each action.  The default way that debug actions are executed are through the OnEndEdit in the text field. When the action finishes, the frame is rendered and all the steps in the lifecycle are executed (https://docs.unity3d.com/Manual/ExecutionOrder.html).  The most important set of events in the lifecycle for us is the Physics events.  If the actions are executed one after another, the loop is skipped.  A few areas where this matters are when you say pick an object up and drop it.  We have logic that waits for the object to settle by allowing one or more frames to pass and then marking the action finished.  Another is more recently we relied on Unity's built in animator to move the arm.  The way it worked was that it would only calculate the next position of the joints once per frame - so you had to let a frame pass in order for the arm to move.  You don't have to use the same ExecuteBatch function, if you want to create a new one that takes a JArray or List<DynamicServerAction> and does a similar thing that is fine with me.

@jjyao @clarkzinzow how about expressing the typing in a more loose way? It seems to me that trying to express bound vs unbound aggregates is just making things more confusing. Furthermore, what's valid seems to differ based on simple vs arrow block. I think just taking `Aggregation = Union[str, AggregateFn]` would be sufficient.

Instead, we can raise good errors (e.g., "You must specify the field to sum over (e.g., Sum("foo")) when using the list aggregate syntax.") in all the relevant cases.

```

class Dataset:
    def aggregate(spec: Union[List[Aggregation], Dict[str, Aggregation]])

# Simple dataset
ds.aggregate(["sum", Max(), Min(lambda x: x + 1)])

# Tabular dataset
ds.aggregate([Sum("f1"), Max("f2"), Min(lambda x: x["f1"])])
ds.aggregate({"f1": [Sum(), "min"], "f2": [Max()]})
```

> So I guess the question for the maintainers is:
> 
> * replace and warn about an existing `~\pydistutils.cfg` file? (less effort, may impact some users)
>   or
> * try and merge new settings into an existing `~\pydistutils.cfg` file? (more effort and failure cases)
>   or
> * propose a new environment-based API to setuptools to provide settings? (takes more time)

So, I'm a little torn here. Pragmatically speaking, the `~\pydistutils.cfg` file would probably work in 99% of situations. But it does feel like a bit of a risk, leaking state into the filesystem like this. Imagine a user does a local build using cibuildwheel on their dev machine, then the computer BSOD or there's a power outage. Their machine isn't going to work for building binary wheels until they find and delete this file.

IMO, getting this into an environment variable would be vastly preferred. Would that be a lot more work, do you think? I wonder if @abravalheri might have an opinion on this, one way or the other? This is looking close.

@prha brought up that fetching all the asset events could be expensive in some situations. E.g. imagine an AND sensor that depends on two assets: an asset that gets updated every minute and an asset that gets updated every month. By the end of the month, every tick would be pulling out 24 * 30 * 60 materializations from the DB.

A way to avoid this issue would be to create a MultiAssetSensorContext and replace the "asset_events" argument with methods on the context. Different methods could return the latest materialization or all materializations, depending on what the user cares about.  Thoughts?

Lastly, a few cosmetic things to think about with the "asset_events" name:
- If feasible, we should find a way to name things so that it's clear to users whether the dictionary includes keys for assets with no events
- Should it be `asset_materializations`?
